phrasal_verbs.meaning: stop asking for something or stop saying that you will do something, because a lot of people oppose you
if someone or something blends in, they are similar to the other people, objects, buildings, etc around them, and so they seem appropriate or you do not notice them
make something happen, especially to cause changes in a situation
start dealing with someone or something much more strictly
be elected for a political job
not be punished severely or at all for something you have been accused of in court; have a particular period of time as a holiday; send something, for example in the post
stop competing or arguing and accept that you cannot win; if you give in to something, you can no longer control the feeling of wanting it
criticise someone who has criticised you; deliberately hurt someone who has hurt you
put someone in a prison; lock all the doors and windows of a building so that no one can get in
decide not to take part in something or stop taking part in it
keep telling someone what to do in an unfair or unpleasant way
choose one person from a group for special attention
not allow yourself to be treated badly, especially by someone in authority
take control of something; begin to do something that someone else was doing
talk to someone as if you think they are not as clever or important as you are

right: give sb the right to do
sentence: serve a sentence (of five years, etc)
subject: subject sb to
bring up / get onto a subject
drop/change the subject
subject to
the subject of
British subject

idioms.idiom: bury your head in the sand
idioms.meaning: ignore a problem or an unpleasant situation and hope that it will disappear
gain/get/have/take control or advantage over a person or situation
be allowed to have or do what you want
used for saying that you should accept other people’s beliefs and way of life, even if they are very different from your own
use your influence in order to get something you want or to help someone, especially when this is unfair
documents, rules or processes that cause delays

idioms.idiom: take the law into your own hands
idioms.meaning: punish someone in your own way without involving the police or the courts, often by doing something illegal yourself
the people who control a situation

D.passage:
Original passage for Power and Social Issues

Tom joined a online lesson because the group wanted fresh practice for course practice. The tutor wrote (1) ...... (2) ...... (3) ...... (4) ...... (5) ...... on the board and asked everyone to explain their choices. Later, the group compared (6) ...... (7) ...... (8) ...... (9) ...... (10) ...... with the rule from the lesson. The text is new, but it practises the same course practice.
